questions.description: A statutory predecessor of section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 has been described as giving rise to a statutory…
questions.text: A statutory predecessor of section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 has been described as giving rise to a statutory contract of the most sacred character." You are required to discuss this statement, explaining the nature and effect of section 31 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the "Bank") to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the "Company") on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair-trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for €2,500,000 in 2007. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1,500,000 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing to the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company and whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer, you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as…
questions.text: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance." You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant cases and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by Simon although Paul has worked in the Company for 10 years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. There are three other directors (all family members, making four directors in total). Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company, approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been "double-jobbing" for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Specifically, Simon has heard that: Jackie has been buying cars from Paul that were traded in-to the Company at a discounted price before they sell them on for a significant profit on their website; and Paul has been using the Company's garage-workshop to service the cars late at night and on Sundays when the garage-workshop is closed. He has used spare parts belonging to the Company but has not reimbursed the Company for these. You are required to advise Simon on the following: (a) whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Paul, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; [and]{.underline} (b) the remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breach of duties. In your answer you must cite all relevant cases and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line wine shop operating from… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line wine shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the "Bank") secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the Bank can freeze at its discretion. Both charges were created in 2016. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which then appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Following his appointment, the Company's liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: (a) through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under section 409 of the Companies Act 2014; and (b) the charge over the book debts was registered under section 409; however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was not registered under section 409 and the liquidator tells you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of the sum due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) [which must be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks]{.underline}. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob's Burger Bars Limited (the "Company") operates a mobile burger… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob's Burger Bars Limited (the "Company") operates a mobile burger business which has six vans on the road, selling burgers and other fast food at events in the greater Dublin region. It owns a premises on the Long Mile Road in Dublin where it pre-prepares some food and stores ingredients etc. The Company employs 15 people and has been in business for some 10 years. The Company's sole shareholder and sole director, Bob, was contacted by the Company's bank this morning and was informed that it was freezing the Company's accounts because it had learned that the Company had been struck off the register almost a year ago and had been dissolved. Bob contacts you in a panic and admits that he may have been remiss in arranging for the filing of the Company's annual return and financial statements. You are required to advise Bob on: (a) How the company came to be struck off the register and how it can be restored to the register; and (b) What are the legal consequences of the company being struck off the register and of the Company being restored to the register. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of ultra vires has been abolished for LTDs, to which…
questions.text: "Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of ultra vires has been abolished for LTDs, to which Parts 2 to 15 applies, but remains in place for DACs, PLCs, CLGs and UCs". You are required to discuss and explain this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While a creditor of a company is entitled ex debito justiciae to a winding-up order, the remedy of winding up is a…
questions.text: "While a creditor of a company is entitled ex debito justiciae to a winding-up order, the remedy of winding up is a discretionary remedy and is not available in cases of disputed debts". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "The key to a successful defence to an application to have a director restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act…
questions.text: "The key to a successful defence to an application to have a director restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 is to establish that the director acted honestly, responsibly and cooperated with the liquidator". You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as…
questions.text: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance". You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: "In addition to actual or deemed authority, the authority to bind a company can arise through a director having…
questions.text: "In addition to actual or deemed authority, the authority to bind a company can arise through a director having ostensible authority and third parties seeking to enforce contracts against companies entered into by directors may also rely upon the Rule in Turquand's case". You are required to discuss this statement in the context of section 40 of the Companies Act 2014, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "A statutory predecessor of section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 has been described as giving rise to a statutory…
questions.text: "A statutory predecessor of section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 has been described as giving rise to a statutory contract of the of the most sacred character". You are required to discuss this statement, explaining the nature and effect of section 31 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner,… Company.
questions.text: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods from wood for the tourist market. He employs six people, and his business has been very successful. Sean comes to you for advice and during the course of the consultation he tells you a number of things about his business. - He is considering incorporating the business but is not sure of the advantages and disadvantages of doing this. - He is about to acquire a new factory outlet, whereby the products will be made in the same location as they are sold and to where members of the public can come and see him and his employees at work. Sean will need to borrow from his bank to acquire the new premises. - Recently, one of his employees received a nasty cut from a chisel which severed an artery and caused him to be off work for two months and although Sean's insurance company paid out the claim, Sean had a few sleepless nights worrying over the claim until he received their letter confirming it was covered in full by the policy. - Sean's longest serving employee, Fred, has for some time been pressing Sean to form a partnership with him so he can share in the success of the business. While Sean is agreeable to giving Fred a slice of the action, he is uncomfortable with forming a partnership with Fred. - While Sean has a bookkeeper, he has never bothered having his accounts audited. You are required to advise Sean on the [advantages and disadvantages]{.underline} of incorporating his business as a private company limited by shares under Part 2 of the Companies Act 2014, having particular regard to the matters raised by Sean during the consultation. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statue law.

questions.description: Freddie is a sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Limited (the "Company") which operates a pet shop…
questions.text: Freddie is a sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Limited (the "Company") which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company's business has been going downhill and Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: a. He removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company's premises and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare, although it doesn't appear that Freda paid for the stock; b. He caused the Company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to him under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to the two dispositions identified at (a) and (b). In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: The liquidator of Timber products Limited (the "Company) which was… Company.
questions.text: The liquidator of Timber products Limited (the "Company) which was incorporated under the Companies Act 2014 contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has just been appointed to the Company by the High Court on the petition of the Revenue Commissioners and needs your help in unravelling the Company's affairs and determining the order of priorities. Specifically, you are advised that: a. A company called Raw Materials Limited has a retention of title clause in its contract for the sale of three batches of timber it supplied to the Company worth €75,000. The liquidator advises you that there is only one batch of timber untouched as one was made into furniture and remains on the Company's premises whereas the third batch was made into furniture and sold. The clause in question provided "the property in the timber shall not transfer to the Company until all sums owing to the Vendor have been paid. In addition, until the timber has been paid for, any goods made from the timber shall become the property of the Vendor. Furthermore, where the timber has been used to make goods and is sold, the Vendor is entitled to the proceeds of the sale of the goods made from the timber which shall be held in trust by the Company for the Vendor". The liquidator wants to know whether he must return the timber, the furniture made therefrom and the proceeds of sale of the goods sold, payment for which has just been made to the liquidator. b. A company called brass Fittings Ltd supplied hinges and such fittings to the Company. It obtained security for the goods it supplied to the Company in the form of a fixed charge over the Company's book debts [and]{.underline} a charge over the bank account into which the book debts were to be paid. The charge over the book debts was registered and the liquidator can see that book debts were paid into a restricted bank account as required. The liquidator sees that the charge over the book debts [was]{.underline} registered under section 409; however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was [not]{.underline} registered under section 409 and the liquidator tells you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of the sum due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator in respect of each of the foregoing matters. Your answer should clearly distinguish the two questions asked in (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company")… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the Company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitor's practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to john. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agency. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than john to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below the market rent, he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the best interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked john to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise john as to the following matters: a. Whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014; [AND ]{.underline} b. Whether your answer would be different if the Company's constitution or a shareholders' agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders'' businesses by referring clients to them. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law. You must distinguish your answers to parts (a) and (b) of the question, both of which carry equal marks.
